title: Data Markets and Privacy Externalities
key_mechanisms: A user’s data can reveal information about other users, so when one user shares, she creates a privacy externality on others. This leakage lowers the marginal value of protecting one’s own data, depressing equilibrium data prices and inducing excessive sharing relative to the first best; competition between platforms does not eliminate this force and can worsen welfare. The paper also proposes mediated data-sharing / de-correlation schemes to preserve beneficial data trades while reducing harmful leakage.
payoff_functions: Platform objective under a single platform: minimize \(\sum_{i\in V} \mathbb{E}(\hat x_i(S)-X_i)^2 - \sigma_i^2 + p_i\), where \(\hat x_i(S)\) is the platform’s estimate of user i’s type and \(p_i\) are payments to users. User i’s utility is given by a privacy-cost term and transfer: \(u_i(a_i,a_{-i},p_i)= -v_i I(a_i=1,a_{-i}) a_i\) when sharing and \(0\) when not sharing, net of payment (as described in the text: “The price … represents both direct payment and privacy cost”; the later payoff notation in the paper is consistent with a privacy loss proportional to leaked information \(I(\cdot)\)). The platform’s utility in the competition section is stated as \(U(a,p)=\sum_{i\in V} I_i(a) - \sum_{i\in V:a_i=1} p_i\).
